title: Semantic forget: 'forget everything about X' finds related memories via retrieve() (dense+lexical), not keyword overlap
source: Ash idea 2026-07-13 (memory-curator breakdown; the demoable gem)
current_state: Both forgetting entry points match lexically only. (1) MemoryTools.memory_delete (tools_memory.py:2258): for non-UUID targets, candidates come from memory_read(target, tier='l3') at line 2296 - BM25/LIKE lexical search. (2) scripts/memory_cli.py cmd_forget (~line 123-145): plain word-overlap scoring (q_words & c_words). So 'forget everything about my old internship' misses a memory stored as 'started at Meta in June' - zero shared tokens. Meanwhile retrieve() (T-292) already solves exactly this matching problem (dense cosine + BM25 fusion over L3+L2) but nothing wires it to forgetting. memory_delete's existing safety design is good and must be preserved: soft=True default (invalid_at, recoverable), >3-entries bulk guard requiring force=True, WAL snapshot before modification.
target_state: memory_delete routes non-UUID candidate matching through self.retrieve(target, k=10, tiers=('l3',)) with a score floor (env PI_FORGET_SCORE_FLOOR, default 0.35) so semantically-related-but-lexically-different memories are found. All existing guards unchanged: soft default, bulk-guard confirm flow (which already returns the candidate list for review - this IS the confirmation UX), WAL snapshot, hard delete only with soft=False. cmd_forget in memory_cli gains the same power: use MemoryTools.retrieve when constructible, keep the word-overlap path as offline fallback. Result dict entries include each match's retrieval score so the caller/user sees WHY a memory matched.
migration_plan: 1. In memory_delete's else-branch (line ~2296): replace memory_read(target, tier='l3') with self.retrieve(target, k=10, tiers=('l3',)); filter hits below PI_FORGET_SCORE_FLOOR; keep result shape (id/content/importance/category dicts) so downstream code is untouched.
2. Ensure retrieve() results here exclude already-invalid rows (they do - dense+lex paths filter invalid_at; verify post-T-298 the active_until filter also applies).

3. scripts/memory_cli.py cmd_forget: try MemoryTools(supabase_url='', supabase_key='') .retrieve(query, k=10) for candidates (offline L3 works; embeddings optional - degrades to BM25); fall back to the existing word-overlap loop if retrieve raises. Show score next to each candidate in the confirm prompt.

4. Reproducing test (the proof, mirrors T-292's): seed 'started the summer role at Meta in June' with a mocked embedding; memory_delete('my old internship', ...) with query embedding mocked identical -> entry found and invalidated; assert the OLD lexical path (memory_read) returns nothing for the same target, proving the gap.

5. Guard tests: >3 semantic matches still triggers the force=True guard; soft=True still sets invalid_at not DELETE; test_memory_delete.py existing cases stay green (T-145 regression - empty/zero-match query must never mass-delete).
risk_notes: Semantic matching WIDENS the blast radius of forgetting - that is the feature and the risk. Mitigations already in the design: score floor, k cap, bulk guard >3 requires force, soft-invalidate default (reversible), WAL snapshot. NEVER hard-delete on a semantic match without soft=False AND force=True. T-145's lesson is the exact hazard class here: a low floor + generic query ('stuff') must not match everything - the score floor plus retrieve()'s BM25_FLOOR/stopword guards cover it; add one test with a generic query asserting few/no matches.
